---
canvas:
  published: true
  type: page
title: 'Motor and Servo'
---

## Where we are

In the previous module you built an H-bridge from discrete MOSFETs (Labs 8–9). That exercise was intentional — understanding *why* an H-bridge works, what shoot-through is, and why dead-time matters, is essential background. But a hand-built discrete H-bridge is fragile, component-hungry, and takes up half a breadboard.

**From this module onward we use the BTS7960 integrated motor driver.** It contains all four MOSFETs, the level shifters, shoot-through protection, dead-time generation, and current sensing in a single IC. What took a full breadboard in Lab 9 is now one module.

## What we cover

This module has four labs:

| Lab | Topic |
|---|---|
| **Lab 10** | Servo motor — theory, signal format, calibration |
| **Lab 11** | Servo library and modular code structure |
| **Lab 12** | Brushed DC motor with the BTS7960 driver |

## Why servo first?

The servo is a self-contained, forgiving actuator — it has its own controller inside and only needs a PWM signal. Starting here lets you practice the LEDC peripheral and get comfortable with the oscilloscope on a clean, easy-to-observe signal before dealing with the higher currents and inductance of the brushed motor.

The servo will also become the **steering actuator** in the RC car project, so calibrating it correctly now pays off later.

Lab 11 then focuses on structuring the code so that servo control, motor control, and wireless communication can all coexist without turning into spaghetti — an investment that pays off directly in the project phase.
